title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward
summary: This paper surveys the emerging field of "agent skills" for large language models, which allows LLMs to dynamically extend capabilities through modular, on-demand packages of instructions and code. It provides a comprehensive overview of architectural foundations, skill acquisition methods, deployment challenges, and critical security concerns, including a proposed governance framework. The work also identifies key open challenges for realizing trustworthy and self-improving skill ecosystems.
key_findings: Agent skills enable dynamic capability extension for LLMs without retraining, through composable packages of instructions, code, and resources.

The field is organized along four axes: architectural foundations (e.g., SKILL$.$md, MCP), skill acquisition (e.g., RL, autonomous discovery), deployment at scale (e.g., CUA stack, benchmarks), and security.
A significant security vulnerability exists, with 26.1% of community-contributed skills empirically found to contain vulnerabilities.
A Skill Trust and Lifecycle Governance Framework is proposed, featuring a four-tier, gate-based permission model to address security risks.
Seven open challenges are identified, including cross-platform skill portability and the development of capability-based permission models.
methodology: This paper employs a survey methodology, providing a comprehensive treatment and organization of the rapidly evolving agent skills landscape.
bias_disclaimer: AI-generated analysis may contain inaccuracies or reflect biases from the underlying model. Always perform critical evaluation.
